old_claim.note: Early combines were horse-drawn and built on prior animal-powered grain-processing practices.

new_claim_absent: No Raemkai donkey-treading dependency remains because horse traction moved early combines but did not make the specific Egyptian hoof-threshing method their documented technical predecessor.

source_shape.source_locator: The combine entry defines a machine that cuts, threshes, and cleans grain and notes early horse-drawn machines without tracing them to Egyptian donkey treading.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Animal traction propelled early combines, while the machine's internal threshing process is the relevant functional component.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Sharing animal power does not establish transmission of the Raemkai floor method into nineteenth-century combination harvesters.

ontology_before: A shared motive-power category was mistaken for a technical lineage from ancient floor treading to a mechanized combine.
ontology_after: The combine keeps its direct threshing-and-winnowing process dependency without a specific unsupported Egyptian lineage.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: combine_harvesters no longer directly depends on animal_powered_threshing.
would_reject_if: A reliable history of the combine demonstrated direct design transmission from donkey-treading threshing floors into early combine threshing mechanisms.
short_reason: Horse-drawn motion does not make donkey floor-treading a combine design predecessor.
